start.title: A Danish Boy In Theresienstadt
start.subtitle: A Drawn Testimony
start.text:
On October 2, 1943, eight-year-old Steen Metz and his parents were arrested in their home in Odense, Denmark, and deported to Theresienstadt Concentration Camp.
What followed were eighteen months of hunger, brutality, and loss—including the death of his father from starvation. Of the 15,000 children who passed through Theresienstadt, fewer than 1,500 survived. Steen was one of them. For decades, like many Holocaust survivors, Steen kept his experiences private. But in 2011, he was ready to share his story through his memoir, A Danish Boy in Theresienstadt.
Now, we retell Steen's journey through an interactive visual experience, where hand-drawn illustrations guide you through his memories. This isn't just a story to read—it's a story to witness, to explore, and to remember.

steenphoto_x5F_group.title: Steen Metz
steenphoto_x5F_group.text: This photograph shows Steen Axel Metz holding ice cream. He is photographed in a park the summer before he and his family are arrested.

familyphoto_x5F_group.title: Family Memories
familyphoto_x5F_group.subtitle: Living at Stovesberg
familyphoto_x5F_group.text: This photograph shows Steen Axel Metz with his father, Axel Mogens Metz, and his grandmother. They are seated on a stone wall in front of their apartment on the Hjallesevej in Odense. Steen has fond memories of his father, who taught him to cycle and with whom he worked in their garden. The garden was not part of the Stovesberg complex. Instead, they worked in a garden around the corner of the street:

transport.title: Transport to Theresienstadt
transport.subtitle: A Three-Day Journey
transport.text: After Steen and his parents were arrested they were loaded onto a cattle car and transported to Theresienstadt. Click on the interactive objects to find out more.

cattlecar_x5F_group.title: The Cattle Car
cattlecar_x5F_group.subtitle: Transport to Theresienstadt
cattlecar_x5F_group.text:
After their arrest, Steen and his parents were loaded into a crowded cattle car. Historians often use the term boxcar, but as Steen notes, survivors frequently call it a cattle car, because that is what it was. He recalls the horrible smell, a result of having no bathroom facilities. The door was locked from the outside, and there was only one stop, during which they received a small amount of water.

Steen and his parents spent three days in complete uncertainty and darkness. There were four children, including Steen, who were able to lie down in the car. The adults had to alternate between sitting and standing. Steen remembers the frightened faces around him; no one knew what was going to happen.

roof_x5F_group.title: Station Bauschowitz-Theresienstadt
roof_x5F_group.subtitle: Transport to Theresienstadt
roof_x5F_group.text:
There was no train station in Theresienstadt. Instead, Steen and his family were transported to the station Bauschowitz-Theresienstadt, known today as Bohušovice train station. From here, after their long journey, they had to walk one and a half miles to Theresienstadt.

Upon arrival, they had to hand over their valuable items, including the jewellery and money they had been encouraged to bring. Steen’s mother was allowed to keep her wedding ring only because the Nazis were unable to remove it from her finger. Although new prisoners were split into groups of women, men, and children after their arrival, Steen was able to stay with his mother in Theresienstadt.

soccer.title: Playing Soccer
soccer.subtitle: Life in Theresienstadt
soccer.text:
Steen was only nine years old when he was in Theresienstadt. As a young boy, he spent his time delivering messages in the camp and playing soccer with the other children.

Click on the interactive objects to find out more.

ball_x5F_group.title: The Soccer Field
ball_x5F_group.subtitle: Playing Soccer
ball_x5F_group.text: There was no goal post, no soccer field, no soccer ball. Instead, Steen and his Czech friends played on a gravel field, and his mother made a ball out of old rags – one that did not last long.

steenmetz_x5F_group.title: Friendships in Theresienstadt
steenmetz_x5F_group.subtitle: Playing Soccer
steenmetz_x5F_group.text: Besides lining up for food and doing voluntary work, Steen played soccer with some of the Czech children in Theresienstadt. To communicate with them, he learned Czech phonetically. Steen recalls that these were the only moments where he felt closest to being a nine-year-old.

friends_x5F_group.title: Friendships in Theresienstadt
friends_x5F_group.subtitle: Playing Soccer
friends_x5F_group.text: One day, when Steen went to the gravel field where they played soccer, his Czech friends did not show up. Steen wondered what was going on and why they did not come. His mother comforted him; she knew what had happened but protected him from the truth. The children had been transported to extermination camps.

whitebus.title: Happier Times
whitebus.subtitle: Liberation by the White Busses
whitebus.text:
The Danish Jews were liberated on the 15th of April by the White Busses.

Click on the interactive objects to find out more.

whitebus_x5F_group.title: Happier Times
whitebus_x5F_group.subtitle: Liberation by the White Busses
whitebus_x5F_group.text:
On April 13th, 1945, a few weeks before the end of the wars, the prisoners in Theresienstadt heard rumours that the White Busses were on their way from Sweden to liberate the Danish Jews. Although Sweden was a neutral country, the White Busses arrived safely in Theresienstadt two days later, on the 15th of April. It was a miracle that the White Busses had been able to move through Germany safely to rescue approximately 425 surviving Danish Jews.

Before their departure, eight young Danish men married women from other countries so they could also be liberated. Among the liberated Jews, there were four babies that were born in secrecy. On the way back, the White Busses had to make several stops. Steen still remembers the bombardments and the faces of young soldiers along the way.

crowd_x5F_group.title: Happier Times
crowd_x5F_group.subtitle: Liberation by the White Busses
crowd_x5F_group.text:
Even though Denmark was still occupied when the White Busses reached the border on the 17th of April, the rescued Jews were greeted with flowers, candy, snacks, and sandwiches. The White Busses travelled on to Sweden, where the liberated prisoners had to spent about eight days in quarantine.

On May 5th, 1945, only two weeks after their arrival in Sweden, the war ends, and Denmark is liberated. Known today in Denmark as Liberation Day, this historic day coincided with Steen’s 10th birthday. Steen and his mother stayed in Sweden for a few weeks before moving back to Odense in early June. After his return, Steen recalls telling his friends from school that they had raised the Danish flags for his birthday.

pavillion_x5F_group.title: A Visit from the Danish Delegation
pavillion_x5F_group.subtitle: The Beautification Program
